# Agentic Tooling: User Stories (Runtime's Perspective)

64 user stories written from the agentic runtime's-eye view — the harness/orchestrator that mediates between agents and tools (scheduling, context, permissions, concurrency, budget, resilience). Third in the set alongside [agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md) (the calling agent's perspective) and [agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-tool-pov.md) (the tool's own perspective).

## Agent Lifecycle & Scheduling

1. As the runtime, I want to spawn sub-agents with an explicit parent-child link, so I can trace any action back to the task that ultimately caused it.
2. As the runtime, I want to cap concurrent agent count per session, so a runaway fan-out doesn't exhaust compute or hit provider rate limits silently.
3. As the runtime, I want to detect when a spawned agent has gone idle or stuck, so I can surface it to the user rather than let it burn budget silently forever.
4. As the runtime, I want a uniform interface for agent-type resolution, so adding a new specialized agent doesn't require touching every call site.
5. As the runtime, I want background agents to notify their caller on completion, rather than requiring the caller to poll, so I don't waste cycles on status checks that haven't changed.
6. As the runtime, I want to let a user interrupt or skip a running agent mid-task, so a bad direction doesn't have to run to completion before it can be corrected.
7. As the runtime, I want an agent's model/effort override to be explicit and logged, so a silent mismatch between what was requested and what ran doesn't go unnoticed.
8. As the runtime, I want to enforce a hard ceiling on total agents spawned per workflow, so a logic bug that spawns in a loop can't run away unbounded.

## Context & Memory Management

9. As the runtime, I want to compact conversation history automatically as it approaches the context limit, so a long-running task doesn't hit a hard wall mid-work.
10. As the runtime, I want compaction to preserve the task's active plan and unresolved obligations, so summarization doesn't silently drop what still needs doing.
11. As the runtime, I want a durable, file-based memory store that survives across sessions, so useful facts don't have to be rediscovered every conversation.
12. As the runtime, I want memory writes distinguished by type (user, feedback, project, reference), so retrieval can be scoped to what's actually relevant instead of dumping everything into context.
13. As the runtime, I want stale memory entries flagged for re-verification against current state, so a fact frozen in time isn't treated as still true.
14. As the runtime, I want deferred tool schemas loaded lazily and cached per session, so every agent doesn't pay the same discovery cost from scratch.
15. As the runtime, I want the token budget for a turn to be trackable and enforceable, so a user's scale directive is a hard ceiling, not a suggestion.
16. As the runtime, I want cache-friendly context boundaries respected, so scheduling decisions don't silently blow the prompt cache.

## Tool Registration & Mediation

17. As the runtime, I want a single source of truth for which tools are available to which agent type, so permission boundaries can't drift between config and what actually executes.
18. As the runtime, I want to validate a tool's declared schema against its actual runtime behavior at registration time, so a stale or wrong schema fails fast instead of confusing every agent that calls it.
19. As the runtime, I want to mediate every tool call through one permission-check chokepoint, so no code path can bypass the confirmation flow for a destructive action.
20. As the runtime, I want to expose MCP server instructions to agents transparently, so agent behavior is driven by the same guidance a human integrator sees, not a hidden default.
21. As the runtime, I want to detect when a tool handle from a previous session's ID space is being reused (e.g. stale tab/browser handles), so an agent doesn't silently act on a dead reference.
22. As the runtime, I want to batch tool-schema fetches into one round trip when an agent requests multiple deferred tools at once, so discovery cost doesn't scale linearly with tool count.
23. As the runtime, I want to log which tools go completely unused across sessions, so the available surface can be pruned instead of growing without bound.
24. As the runtime, I want new tool versions introduced without breaking agents mid-task, so a schema change doesn't corrupt an in-flight tool call.

## Permissions, Safety & Sandboxing

25. As the runtime, I want a default-deny posture for destructive operations, so an agent must explicitly earn elevated trust rather than assume it.
26. As the runtime, I want permission grants scoped to the exact action requested, so approving one git push doesn't silently authorize all future pushes.
27. As the runtime, I want hooks to run deterministically on every matching event, so safety policy isn't a suggestion the model can reason its way around.
28. As the runtime, I want to isolate agents that mutate files concurrently into separate worktrees, so parallel work can't silently clobber shared state.
29. As the runtime, I want content fetched from external sources treated as untrusted input, flagged distinctly from user instructions, so injected text can't masquerade as user intent.
30. As the runtime, I want a way to run an agent fully sandboxed against non-production resources, so a plan can be validated without risking real side effects.
31. As the runtime, I want every destructive tool call gated by an explicit user-facing confirmation, unless the user has pre-authorized that exact class of action.
32. As the runtime, I want to surface which specific rule or hook blocked an action, so the user can adjust configuration instead of the agent blindly retrying.

## Concurrency, Isolation & Resource Management

33. As the runtime, I want a global concurrency cap on simultaneous tool/agent calls, so a large fan-out degrades into a queue instead of overwhelming downstream services.
34. As the runtime, I want per-session scratch directories that are isolated and auto-namespaced, so parallel sessions never collide on temp files.
35. As the runtime, I want worktree isolation to be opt-in and cost-aware, so its overhead is only paid when agents genuinely need conflict-free parallel mutation.
36. As the runtime, I want to auto-clean an isolated worktree if the agent made no changes, so idle isolation doesn't leave disk debris behind.
37. As the runtime, I want to detect resource contention (two agents editing the same file) before it happens, not just after a conflict is discovered.
38. As the runtime, I want background task output retrievable after the fact even if the caller wasn't actively watching, so a completed result is never silently lost.
39. As the runtime, I want cross-agent shared state (a budget counter, an agent-count cap) to be atomic, so concurrent spawns can't race past a limit.
40. As the runtime, I want long-running or hung processes killable without corrupting the session's overall state.

## Observability, Audit & Debugging

41. As the runtime, I want every agent's actual tool calls and results persisted to a journal, so a resumed or debugged run can be reconstructed from ground truth, not from a summary.
42. As the runtime, I want to distinguish an agent's self-reported summary from its verified actions, so "I did X" claims can be checked against what actually happened.
43. As the runtime, I want structured, greppable logs per agent invocation, so diagnosing a failure doesn't require re-running the whole workflow.
44. As the runtime, I want to expose live progress (phase, current agent, status) to the user during a multi-agent run, so a long operation doesn't look like silence.
45. As the runtime, I want telemetry on reasoning-effort and model choice per call, so cost/quality tradeoffs are visible and tunable after the fact.
46. As the runtime, I want to replay a prior run from its journal without recomputing already-cached agent calls, so fixing one broken step doesn't force a full re-run.
47. As the runtime, I want errors from a terminal API failure distinguishable from a logical agent failure, so retries target the right layer.
48. As the runtime, I want any silently-dropped or truncated work (capped top-N, skipped items) logged explicitly, so partial coverage is never mistaken for complete coverage.

## Cost, Budget & Rate Limiting

49. As the runtime, I want a shared token-budget pool visible across the main loop and all spawned workflows, so nested spawns can't quietly exceed a stated ceiling.
50. As the runtime, I want to reject further agent spawns once a hard budget ceiling is hit, rather than let the last spawn silently overrun it.
51. As the runtime, I want cost estimates surfaced before launching an expensive multi-agent operation, so the user can approve the scale before it runs, not after the bill arrives.
52. As the runtime, I want per-provider rate limits tracked centrally, so independent agents don't each discover the same 429 the hard way.
53. As the runtime, I want idle or stuck background agents to eventually time out, so a hung call doesn't accumulate cost indefinitely unnoticed.
54. As the runtime, I want scaling decisions (fleet size, depth of a loop) able to key off remaining budget dynamically, not just a fixed count set in advance.
55. As the runtime, I want the marginal cost of "just one more agent" visible before it's spent, so fan-out doesn't grow past what the task actually warranted.
56. As the runtime, I want billing-relevant operations (cloud review, remote execution) to require explicit opt-in, so cost is never incurred on the user's behalf without a clear ask.

## Resilience, Resumability & Versioning

57. As the runtime, I want a workflow run resumable from any prior point, with unchanged prior steps served from cache, so an edit to one stage doesn't force redoing the whole run.
58. As the runtime, I want session state to survive a crash or restart, so in-progress multi-step work isn't lost to an unrelated failure.
59. As the runtime, I want scheduled/looping tasks to have both a primary wake signal and a long fallback heartbeat, so the loop survives if the primary signal never fires.
60. As the runtime, I want non-deterministic operations (current time, randomness) disallowed inside resumable scripts, so a replay produces the same plan as the original run.
61. As the runtime, I want tool and prompt versions pinned per run, so an in-flight task isn't silently altered by an unrelated deploy mid-execution.
62. As the runtime, I want a clear migration path when a tool's schema changes, so agents mid-task aren't broken by a contract shift they didn't opt into.
63. As the runtime, I want failure of one item in a large batch isolated, so one bad input doesn't poison results for everything else in the run.
64. As the runtime, I want a documented, versioned changelog of runtime behavior changes, so a user's mental model of "how this works" doesn't silently go stale.
